text: TINOCO ARBITRATION (Great Britain v. Costa Rica, 1923). FACTS: The Tinoco regime seized power in Costa Rica in 1917 and governed until 1919. During that period it issued concessions and banknotes. After the regime fell, the successor Costa Rican government repudiated those obligations, arguing that the Tinoco government had never been recognised by Great Britain, the United States, or other powers. HOLDING: Chief Justice William Taft (sole arbitrator) held for Great Britain. Non-recognition by foreign states does not extinguish the legal personality of a de facto government. The Tinoco regime was the effective government; its acts bind the successor state. LEGAL PRINCIPLE: A de facto government that effectively controls a state is that state for purposes of international law, regardless of whether foreign powers formally recognise it. A successor government cannot repudiate the legally binding acts of its predecessor merely because that predecessor lacked international recognition. SIGNIFICANCE: Foundational authority on de facto government recognition and state succession.

metadata.citation: Great Britain v. Costa Rica (1923) 1 RIAA 369

text: ISLAND OF PALMAS ARBITRATION (Netherlands v. United States, 1928, Arbitrator Max Huber). FACTS: Dispute over sovereignty of the Island of Palmas (now Miangas) between the Dutch East Indies (Netherlands) and the Philippines (United States). The US derived its claim from the 1898 Treaty of Paris ceding the Philippines from Spain; the Netherlands claimed through continuous and peaceful exercise of state authority. HOLDING: Max Huber awarded sovereignty to the Netherlands. Discovery alone, without subsequent effective occupation, does not establish a continuing title to territory. The Netherlands had continuously and peacefully displayed state authority over the island over a long period. LEGAL PRINCIPLE: Effective and continuous display of state authority (effectivités) prevails over a title based on mere discovery where the discoverer fails to follow up with actual occupation and administration. Title must be maintained; it cannot rest on historic discovery alone. SIGNIFICANCE: Leading authority on territorial sovereignty, effectivités doctrine, and the critical date principle.

metadata.citation: Netherlands v. United States (1928) 2 RIAA 829

text: TRAIL SMELTER ARBITRATION (United States v. Canada, 1941). FACTS: A smelter in Trail, British Columbia, emitted sulphur dioxide fumes that caused damage to crops, land, and business in Washington State, USA. HOLDING: The Tribunal held that Canada was responsible for the transboundary air pollution and ordered cessation plus reparations. LEGAL PRINCIPLE: No state has the right to use or permit the use of its territory in such a manner as to cause injury by fumes in or to the territory of another or the properties or persons therein when the case is of serious consequence and the injury is established by clear and convincing evidence. SIGNIFICANCE: Foundational authority for the no-harm principle in international environmental law; cited as customary international law in subsequent treaties and the Stockholm Declaration (Principle 21).

metadata.citation: United States v. Canada (1941) 3 RIAA 1905

text: ALABAMA CLAIMS ARBITRATION (United States v. Great Britain, 1872). FACTS: During the American Civil War, Britain permitted Confederate warships including the CSS Alabama to be built and fitted out in British ports. These vessels caused extensive damage to Union shipping. After the war, the US claimed Britain had violated its duties of neutrality. HOLDING: The Tribunal awarded $15.5 million in gold to the United States. Britain had failed to exercise due diligence as a neutral to prevent the departure of the warships. LEGAL PRINCIPLE: A neutral state is under a duty of due diligence to prevent the fitting out or arming of vessels in its ports intended to cruise against states with which it is at peace. Failure to exercise such diligence attracts state responsibility for resulting damage. SIGNIFICANCE: Established the due diligence standard for neutral state responsibility; early landmark in international arbitration procedure.

metadata.citation: United States v. Great Britain (1872)

text: CHORZOW FACTORY CASE (Germany v. Poland, PCIJ, 1927-1928). FACTS: Poland expropriated a nitrate factory in Upper Silesia that had been operated by Germany contrary to the provisions of the Geneva Convention 1922. HOLDING: The PCIJ held that Poland had violated the Convention and was obliged to make reparation. LEGAL PRINCIPLE: It is a principle of international law that the breach of an engagement involves an obligation to make reparation in an adequate form. Reparation must, as far as possible, wipe out all the consequences of the illegal act and re-establish the situation which would, in all probability, have existed if that act had not been committed. SIGNIFICANCE: Foundational authority for the law of state responsibility and the reparations standard; Article 31 of the ILC Articles on State Responsibility codifies this principle.

metadata.citation: Germany v. Poland, PCIJ Series A No. 17 (1928)

text: NOTTEBOHM CASE (Liechtenstein v. Guatemala, ICJ, 1955). FACTS: Friedrich Nottebohm was born in Germany, lived in Guatemala for 34 years, and obtained Liechtenstein nationality in 1939. Guatemala seized his property during WWII. Liechtenstein brought a claim of diplomatic protection on his behalf. HOLDING: The ICJ rejected the claim, finding that Nottebohm lacked a genuine link with Liechtenstein. LEGAL PRINCIPLE: Nationality for purposes of diplomatic protection must be based on a genuine connection between the person and the state — the bond of nationality must correspond to a factual situation. A state granting nationality without a genuine link cannot rely on that nationality against other states. SIGNIFICANCE: Leading case on nationality and genuine link doctrine in international law.

metadata.citation: Liechtenstein v. Guatemala, ICJ Reports 1955

text: CORFU CHANNEL CASE (United Kingdom v. Albania, ICJ, 1949). FACTS: British warships passing through the Corfu Channel were damaged by mines laid in Albanian territorial waters. Albania denied knowledge of the mines. HOLDING: The ICJ held Albania responsible because it knew of the mines and failed to warn shipping, even if it did not lay them itself. LEGAL PRINCIPLE: Every state has an obligation not to allow knowingly its territory to be used for acts contrary to the rights of other states. States have a duty to notify and warn in circumstances where they have knowledge of danger to international navigation. SIGNIFICANCE: Established the general obligation not to allow one's territory to be used for acts harmful to other states; foundational for the no-harm principle.

metadata.citation: United Kingdom v. Albania, ICJ Reports 1949, p. 4

text: NICARAGUA v. UNITED STATES (Military and Paramilitary Activities, ICJ, 1986). FACTS: Nicaragua brought proceedings alleging that the United States had supported Contra rebels and mined Nicaraguan harbours. HOLDING: The ICJ found the US in breach of the prohibition on the use of force and the principle of non-intervention. The effective control test was applied to assess attribution of Contra acts to the US. LEGAL PRINCIPLE: Customary international law prohibits the threat or use of force against the territorial integrity of any state. For conduct of an irregular armed group to be attributed to a state, that state must exercise effective control over the specific operation. Collective self-defence requires prior armed attack and a request from the victim state. SIGNIFICANCE: Defines the effective control test for attribution; clarifies the conditions for lawful collective self-defence.

metadata.citation: Nicaragua v. United States, ICJ Reports 1986, p. 14

text: LOTUS CASE (France v. Turkey, PCIJ, 1927). FACTS: A French vessel, the SS Lotus, collided with a Turkish vessel on the high seas, causing deaths. Turkey arrested and tried the French officer on board. France protested that Turkey lacked jurisdiction. HOLDING: The PCIJ held that Turkey had not violated international law by exercising jurisdiction. States are free to exercise jurisdiction unless a prohibitive rule exists. LEGAL PRINCIPLE: International law does not prohibit a state from exercising jurisdiction on its own territory unless a prohibitive rule can be shown to exist. The permissive principle: everything not prohibited is permitted in international law. SIGNIFICANCE: Though controversial and partially superseded by UNCLOS, the Lotus principle remains a reference point for debates on state jurisdiction and the sources of international law obligations.

metadata.citation: France v. Turkey, PCIJ Series A No. 10 (1927)

text: BARCELONA TRACTION CASE (Belgium v. Spain, ICJ, 1970). FACTS: Belgium brought a claim on behalf of Belgian shareholders in a Canadian company (Barcelona Traction) whose assets were declared bankrupt by Spain. HOLDING: The ICJ rejected Belgium's claim, finding that only the state of nationality of the company (Canada) could exercise diplomatic protection on its behalf, not the state of shareholders. LEGAL PRINCIPLE: Obligations erga omnes are obligations of states towards the international community as a whole. They derive from the outlawing of aggression, genocide, slavery, and racial discrimination. Every state has a legal interest in protection from these wrongs. The basic rights of the human person constitute erga omnes obligations. SIGNIFICANCE: Introduced the concept of obligations erga omnes into international law.

metadata.citation: Belgium v. Spain, ICJ Reports 1970, p. 3

text: CAROLINE CASE (1837). FACTS: During the Canadian Rebellion of 1837, British forces crossed into US territory and destroyed the American steamship Caroline, which was being used to supply the rebels. One US citizen was killed. US Secretary of State Webster protested and demanded justification. HOLDING / CORRESPONDENCE: British Foreign Secretary Lord Ashburton acknowledged the principle that self-defence must be shown to have been necessary, with no alternative, instant, and leaving no moment for deliberation, and that nothing unreasonable or excessive was done. LEGAL PRINCIPLE: The Caroline formula establishes the customary law requirements for self-defence: (1) necessity — the threat must be instant, overwhelming, and leaving no choice of means; (2) proportionality — the response must not be unreasonable or excessive. SIGNIFICANCE: The Webster formula remains the foundation of the customary international law test for self-defence, incorporated into Article 51 of the UN Charter analysis.

metadata.citation: The Caroline Affair, 29 B.F.S.P. 1137-1138 (1837)

text: REPARATIONS FOR INJURIES ADVISORY OPINION (ICJ, 1949). FACTS: Following the assassination of UN mediator Count Bernadotte in Israel, the UN General Assembly asked the ICJ whether the UN had capacity to bring an international claim against a state. HOLDING: The ICJ advised that the UN does have international legal personality and can bring claims on behalf of its agents. LEGAL PRINCIPLE: An international organisation may possess international legal personality even without being a state, if its purposes and functions as specified or implied in its constituent instruments and developed in practice necessitate it. Such personality is objective — binding even on non-member states. SIGNIFICANCE: Founding authority for international legal personality of international organisations.

metadata.citation: Reparation for Injuries Suffered in the Service of the United Nations, ICJ Reports 1949, p. 174

text: RAINBOW WARRIOR ARBITRATION (New Zealand v. France, 1990). FACTS: French intelligence agents sank the Greenpeace vessel Rainbow Warrior in Auckland harbour in 1985. France and New Zealand agreed a settlement requiring France to keep the agents on Hao Atoll. France later repatriated the agents on medical grounds without New Zealand's consent. HOLDING: The Tribunal found France in breach of its international obligation. LEGAL PRINCIPLE: Non-compliance with a bilateral settlement agreement constitutes a breach of an international obligation giving rise to state responsibility. The appropriate remedy for breach of a bilateral obligation is satisfaction and compensation. Countermeasures must be proportionate. SIGNIFICANCE: Important case on state responsibility, countermeasures, and the obligation to perform bilateral agreements in good faith.

metadata.citation: New Zealand v. France (Rainbow Warrior) (1990) 20 RIAA 217

text: OIL PLATFORMS CASE (Iran v. United States, ICJ, 2003). FACTS: The United States destroyed Iranian oil platforms in the Persian Gulf in 1987-1988, claiming self-defence after Iranian attacks on neutral shipping. Iran brought a claim under the 1955 Treaty of Amity. HOLDING: The ICJ dismissed Iran's claims on the merits, but held that the US had not proven the conditions for lawful self-defence. LEGAL PRINCIPLE: The right of self-defence under Article 51 of the UN Charter requires an armed attack. The measures taken must be necessary and proportionate. Attacks on commercial shipping do not necessarily constitute an armed attack attributable to a state for purposes of Article 51. SIGNIFICANCE: Clarifies the necessity and proportionality requirements for self-defence under Article 51.

metadata.citation: Iran v. United States, ICJ Reports 2003, p. 161

text: ARMED ACTIVITIES ON THE TERRITORY OF THE CONGO (DRC v. Uganda, ICJ, 2005). FACTS: DRC brought proceedings alleging that Uganda had violated international law by engaging in military operations, supporting rebel groups, and plundering natural resources in DRC territory. HOLDING: The ICJ found Uganda responsible for violations of the prohibition on the use of force, the principle of non-intervention, international humanitarian law, and international human rights law. LEGAL PRINCIPLE: A state is responsible for the actions of its armed forces on foreign territory. Occupation triggers obligations under international humanitarian law. Plunder of natural resources by occupying forces constitutes a violation of international law. SIGNIFICANCE: Consolidates the law on use of force, occupation, and the concurrent application of IHL and IHRL.

metadata.source_name: Armed Activities on the Territory of the Congo
metadata.citation: DRC v. Uganda, ICJ Reports 2005, p. 168

text: LEGALITY OF THE THREAT OR USE OF NUCLEAR WEAPONS (Advisory Opinion, ICJ, 1996). FACTS: The UN General Assembly asked the ICJ whether the threat or use of nuclear weapons is permitted under international law. HOLDING: The Court could not definitively conclude whether the use of nuclear weapons would be lawful or unlawful in extreme circumstances of self-defence where the very survival of a state is at stake, but found that any use must comply with IHL requirements of distinction and proportionality. LEGAL PRINCIPLE: Nuclear weapons are subject to the requirements of international humanitarian law. The threat or use of nuclear weapons is generally contrary to the rules of international law applicable in armed conflict. States are under an obligation to pursue nuclear disarmament in good faith. SIGNIFICANCE: Affirmed the applicability of IHL to nuclear weapons; the non liquet on extreme self-defence remains significant.

metadata.citation: Legality of the Threat or Use of Nuclear Weapons, ICJ Reports 1996, p. 226

text: SOUTH WEST AFRICA / NAMIBIA ADVISORY OPINION (Legal Consequences for States, ICJ, 1971). FACTS: South Africa refused to end its mandate over South West Africa (Namibia) after the UN General Assembly terminated it. The Security Council requested an advisory opinion on the legal consequences. HOLDING: The ICJ advised that South Africa's continued presence in Namibia was illegal and that states were under an obligation not to recognise or assist the illegal occupation. LEGAL PRINCIPLE: A Security Council resolution under Chapter VI declaring a situation illegal creates an obligation on member states not to recognise that situation or render aid to the violating state. The right of self-determination has developed into a principle of international law applicable beyond colonial situations. SIGNIFICANCE: Affirmed the binding legal force of Security Council resolutions and the principle of self-determination.

metadata.citation: Legal Consequences for States of the Continued Presence of South Africa in Namibia, ICJ Reports 1971, p. 16

text: WALL ADVISORY OPINION (Legal Consequences of the Construction of a Wall in the Occupied Palestinian Territory, ICJ, 2004). FACTS: The UN General Assembly requested an advisory opinion on the legal consequences of Israel's construction of a wall/separation barrier in the Occupied Palestinian Territory. HOLDING: The ICJ advised that the wall and its associated regime were contrary to international law; Israel was obliged to cease construction, dismantle existing sections, and make reparations. LEGAL PRINCIPLE: International humanitarian law (Fourth Geneva Convention) and international human rights law apply simultaneously in occupied territories — they are complementary, not mutually exclusive. The right of self-determination applies to the Palestinian people. States are under an obligation not to recognise or assist the maintenance of the illegal situation. SIGNIFICANCE: Affirms the concurrent applicability of IHL and IHRL in occupied territory.

metadata.citation: Legal Consequences of the Construction of a Wall in the Occupied Palestinian Territory, ICJ Reports 2004, p. 136

text: LAGRAND CASE (Germany v. United States, ICJ, 2001). FACTS: Two German nationals (the LaGrand brothers) were executed by the US after being convicted of murder, without being informed of their right to consular assistance under Article 36 of the Vienna Convention on Consular Relations. Germany brought proceedings and requested provisional measures. HOLDING: The ICJ held that the US had violated Article 36 VCCR and that its failure to comply with provisional measures also constituted a breach of international obligations. LEGAL PRINCIPLE: Provisional measures indicated by the ICJ under Article 41 of the Statute are legally binding on the parties. Article 36 VCCR creates individual rights for foreign nationals. Procedural default rules in domestic law cannot be invoked to defeat individual rights under Article 36. SIGNIFICANCE: Established the binding nature of ICJ provisional measures; recognised individual rights under VCCR.

metadata.citation: Germany v. United States, ICJ Reports 2001, p. 466

text: NEER CLAIM (LFH Neer and Pauline Neer v. United Mexican States, 1926). FACTS: An American national (Neer) was killed in Mexico by unknown assailants. The US brought a claim of diplomatic protection, alleging Mexico had failed to investigate properly. HOLDING: The Mexico-US General Claims Commission dismissed the claim. LEGAL PRINCIPLE: The treatment of an alien, to constitute an international delinquency, should amount to an outrage, to bad faith, to wilful neglect of duty, or to an insufficiency of governmental action so far short of international standards that every reasonable and impartial man would readily recognise its insufficiency. This is the international minimum standard of treatment. SIGNIFICANCE: Classic formulation of the minimum standard of treatment for aliens in customary international law.

metadata.citation: LFH Neer and Pauline Neer v. United Mexican States (1926) 4 RIAA 60
